topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The attic air hung thick and dusty, smelling of aged paper and forgotten lavender. I was supposed to be looking for old photo albums, but my fingers kept brushing against something stiff and rectangular tucked beneath a moth-eaten tapestry. It was a stack of papers, neatly bound, each one a meticulously completed essay, a perfect diagram, a flawless poem. My breath hitched in my throat. My mother, who always seemed so composed, had kept every single thing. A strange, hollow feeling settled in my chest, like a sudden drop in temperature. I picked up a test on cellular biology, the ink faded but the handwriting unmistakable. I couldn't seem to focus on anything else; the familiar shape of my own life seemed to have fractured, leaving me adrift in this sudden, overwhelming archive of her meticulousness.

[story 2]

I found the box behind the loose floorboard in the spare room. It wasn't what I expected. Instead of old trinkets, it was a pristine collection of schoolwork, from kindergarten through high school. Each assignment was folded precisely, labeled with faded blue ink. Running a hand over the crisp edges of a history report, a wave of vertigo washed over me. Where did all this come from? My mind felt like a spinning top, the edges blurring into a dizzying haze. I started pacing the small room, my footsteps echoing too loudly on the bare floorboards. "Mom?" I whispered, the sound thin and reedy. The sheer volume of effort, the sheer volume of unseen work, made my head swim.

[story 3]

It was tucked inside a seldom-used drawer in the study, a secret compartment I’d never noticed before. Inside, nestled in tissue paper, was an entire year's worth of homework. The weight of it felt substantial, anchoring me to a reality that suddenly seemed unstable. I stared at the pages—math proofs, literature analyses, science lab reports—and couldn't process the sheer scope of it. My hands started trembling, making it hard to hold the stack steady. I sank onto a nearby armchair, pulling my knees to my chest. Every detail of my recent memory felt slightly skewed, like looking through warped glass. Why was this here? What did it all mean?
